title: Ceasefire Collapse, Fed Divide, Yen at 40-Year Low
subhead: Trump declares Iran ceasefire 'over' as fresh strikes hit southern Iran and Jordan intercepts missiles, while FOMC minutes reveal a Fed deeply split on rate direction and the Japanese yen slides to near four-decade lows against the dollar.

sections.heading: Iran Ceasefire Collapses as Strikes Resume
sections.body:
President Trump declared the US-Iran ceasefire memorandum 'over' at the NATO summit in Ankara on Wednesday, calling Iranian leaders 'scum' and vowing to 'hit them hard again tonight.' The declaration followed a second consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, with American forces hitting more than 80 targets, according to US Central Command. Iran's IRGC responded by firing missiles at US military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Jordan's air defenses intercepted several missiles and drones that entered its airspace on Thursday, marking the conflict's further spillover beyond the Gulf.

Iranian state media reported fresh US strikes overnight targeting areas near Asaluyeh on the Arabian Gulf, home to critical energy infrastructure linked to the South Pars gas field. A US defense official said American forces were not conducting operations at the time of some reported explosions, creating conflicting accounts from Tehran, Washington, and Israel.

The White House reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil sales on Tuesday in retaliation for Iran striking commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz. The 60-day MOU signed June 17 — which traded sanctions relief for safe passage through Hormuz — now appears effectively dead, with Trump threatening to strike civilian infrastructure and potentially seize Kharg Island, Iran's key oil export terminal, according to AP News.
sections.whyItMatters: The return to open hostilities kills the already-fragile Hormuz passage deal, revives risk premiums in energy markets, and raises the prospect of a broader regional conflict drawing in Jordan and Gulf states.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz: No Easy Path Back to Normal
sections.body:
Even before the ceasefire collapsed, the CFR warned that restoring normal traffic through the Strait of Hormuz faced formidable obstacles: sea mines, transit fees Iran intends to charge, infrastructure damage, and deep regional mistrust. The MOU had stipulated Iran would allow ships to pass 'with no charge for 60 days only' and work with Oman to define future administration of the strait — language Tehran seized on to claim an ongoing regulatory role, demanding what could amount to $40 billion a year in transit fees, roughly equal to its prewar oil revenues, according to the Wall Street Journal.

CNBC reports that Brent crude settled at $71.99/bbl on Monday after OPEC+ approved another output target increase of 188,000 bpd from August, on top of similar hikes for June and July. Saudi Arabia slashed its August official selling price for Arab Light crude to Asia to $1.50 below the Oman/Dubai average — the biggest monthly cut since Reuters records began in 2003. The UAE, which quit OPEC to escape production caps, raised output to near record highs above 3.8 million bpd in June. Mizuho's Robert Yawger noted 'it is increasingly looking like the Gulf producers are gearing up for a price war.' Oil prices shot up 5% after Trump said negotiations with Iran were over, according to CBS News, before retreating toward prewar levels as the glut from stranded tankers exiting the Gulf weighed on prices.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran intends to monetize Hormuz transit long-term, transforming a chokepoint into a revenue stream. Meanwhile, Gulf producers are flooding the market, creating a dual dynamic of geopolitical risk premium and supply glut that could whipsaw prices in coming weeks.

sections.heading: FOMC Minutes Expose Deep Divide on Rate Path
sections.body:
Minutes from the June FOMC meeting — the first under Chair Kevin Warsh — reveal a Federal Reserve sharply divided on the direction of interest rates. Almost all 19 FOMC members agreed they should raise rates if prices keep rising, but they also agreed they could hold or even cut if inflation cools. The minutes described 'considerable uncertainty' about the path ahead. Pantheon Macroeconomics' Oliver Allen wrote that 'the FOMC remains genuinely conflicted and uncertain about the best next step for policy.'

Consumer prices are rising at an annual pace of roughly 4%, double the Fed's 2% target, largely driven by the Iran war's impact on energy. Some officials warned that supply disruptions 'could persist longer than currently anticipated' and that the AI infrastructure buildout may 'contribute to more persistent inflationary pressures.' But the June jobs report showed employers adding only about 57,000 positions, down from 148,000 in May — a soft labor market that complicates any case for tightening.

Barclays chief US economist Marc Giannoni sees rate risks 'skewed toward hikes' but expects the Fed to hold all year. CME Group's FedWatch tool shows roughly a 25% chance of a hike at the July 28-29 meeting. BMO's Ian Lyngen wrote that 'nothing within the realized data implies the Committee has a strong sense of urgency to hike rates in July,' suggesting September or October as more likely action dates if inflation persists.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed is in a policy bind: inflation above target argues for hikes, but a weakening labor market and energy price volatility argue for patience. Kalshi traders see roughly 50% odds of a rate hike sometime in 2026, but the near-term path is anything but clear.

sections.heading: Yen Hits Near 40-Year Low as BOJ Hike Fails to Stem Decline
sections.body:
The Japanese yen slid to near 40-year lows against the dollar this week, trading around 162/USD, even after the Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate from 0.75% to 1% — a 31-year high for Japanese interest rates. The yen is set for a weekly decline as intervention risks mount. Reuters reports that Japan may have spent as much as $32 billion on yen-buying intervention in recent sessions, according to US News.

CoinDesk reports that the collapsing yen is pushing Japanese companies and institutions into alternative assets, including Bitcoin and XRP, as a hedge against currency depreciation. Hedge funds have turned the most bearish on the yen since 2007, boosting short bets to nearly 138,000 contracts as of June 30. Japanese government bond yields have also hit multidecade highs, which MSN characterized as a 'coded warning for US stocks' — historically, sharply rising JGB yields have preceded stress in global equity markets.

Across Asia, South Korea vowed to curb excessive won volatility as the currency hit its weakest since 2009, and Indonesia also pledged action, Bloomberg reported. The broad-based dollar strength, fueled by safe-haven flows and the rate differential with still-low-yielding Japan, is pressuring central banks across the region to defend their currencies.
sections.whyItMatters: Despite the BOJ's most aggressive rate hike in decades, the yen continues to crumble, signaling that the interest-rate differential with the US remains overwhelming. Massive intervention and rising regional currency stress raise the risk of competitive dynamics and capital flight from Asian markets.

sections.heading: Trump Halts All Trade With Spain, Threatens French Wine Tariffs
sections.body:
President Trump ordered an immediate halt to all US trade with NATO ally Spain during the Ankara summit, citing Spain's low defense spending and its position on Iran, US News reported. The executive action represents an extraordinary escalation: using trade policy as a blunt instrument against a fellow NATO member during a summit convened to strengthen alliance cohesion.

Separately, Trump threatened a 100% tariff on French wines and champagne unless France scraps its 3% digital services tax on US technology companies, CNBC and France 24 reported. The US already imposes 15% tariffs on French wines, up from 10%. French wine and spirits exports to the US fell 15.9% in value in 2025, to 1.9 billion euros, according to the American Association of Wine Economists. Trump previously threatened 200% tariffs on French wines in January to pressure Macron into joining his Board of Peace initiative.

The moves come as G7 leaders at the Evian summit launched an action plan to secure critical mineral supply chains after China tightened control over rare earth exports. Reuters reported that Trump's critical minerals pricing plan faces skeptical G7 partners and a divided industry.
sections.whyItMatters: Weaponizing trade against allies during a war is a new escalation. Spain is a NATO member; targeting it disrupts alliance unity when cohesion matters most. The French wine threat revives the digital tax conflict that has festered since 2019, while the G7's critical minerals push faces internal fractures.

sections.heading: ECB Accounts Show Inflation Staying High Despite Hikes; Bank of Canada Holds
sections.body:
ECB accounts released July 9 showed policymakers were presented with projections indicating inflation would stay above target into next year, despite the central bank having already raised rates by 25 basis points in June — its first hike since 2023. Latvia's ECB governing council member Martins Kazaks said there was 'no urgency' to hike again in July after Iran peace efforts lowered oil prices, Bloomberg reported. But ECB chief economist Philip Lane said the ECB would remain proactive against high inflation even after the Iran deal, suggesting the bank stands ready to act if price pressures persist.

In Canada, the Bank of Canada held its overnight rate at 2.25% for a fifth consecutive meeting in June, InsideHalton and DailyHive reported. Analysts predict a continued rate freeze as Trump's trade fallout and the oil price spike from the Iran war create conflicting pressures on the Canadian economy. The next BoC decision is July 15.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB faces the same dilemma as the Fed: inflation above target but uncertain forward path. Kazaks' 'no urgency' signal contrasts with Lane's proactive stance, mirroring the Fed's own internal split. For markets, the message from both major central banks is data-dependency, not clear forward guidance.

sections.heading: Gold Pulls Back as Rate-Hike Bets Rise, but Central Banks Keep Buying
sections.body:
Gold prices have declined from record highs above $5,000/oz hit in January, pressured by speculation that the Fed may raise rates this year. USA Today reports that institutional investors have been 'almost instantaneously' dumping gold stored as a hedge against rate cuts, according to advisor Evan Mills. Bank of America cut its 2026 average gold forecast, Reuters reported, though it sees long-term upside.

Yet the pullback has been limited by two countervailing forces: safe-haven demand from the Iran conflict and relentless central bank buying. A SeekingAlpha survey found central bank gold accumulation has doubled over the past four years, with 1,000 tonnes purchased annually since 2020 — up from a 500-tonne average in the prior decade. 89% of respondents expect global reserves to rise further in the next 12 months, and 74% anticipate a moderate or significant decline in US dollar holdings within global reserves over the next five years, favoring gold instead.

The interplay means gold is facing a tug-of-war: rate-hike expectations push it down, but geopolitical risk and official-sector demand push it up. As USA Today noted, 'if the Fed raises rates because inflation remains elevated, investors may still turn to gold as a hedge against uncertainty.'
sections.whyItMatters: The gold market is a real-time gauge of the competing forces shaping global capital flows: monetary tightening versus geopolitical risk versus dedollarization. Central banks are voting with their reserves, and they are voting for gold.

bottomLine: The Iran ceasefire is dead, the Fed is genuinely conflicted, the yen is sliding despite the BOJ's best efforts, and Trump is weaponizing trade against his own allies. The common thread: uncertainty. With oil whipsawing between supply glut and geopolitical shock, with the FOMC split between inflation hawks and labor-market doves, and with the dollar strengthening at the expense of Asian currencies, the rest of July will be shaped by whether Trump escalates further in Iran, whether the July 28-29 FOMC meeting signals a hike, and whether Japan's intervention can finally arrest the yen's decline. Markets are pricing in a rate hike by year-end, but the data — softening jobs, volatile energy — argues for patience. The one clear signal: central banks worldwide are buying gold and diversifying away from the dollar, a trend that will outlast any single news cycle.
sources: AP News, CFR, CNBC, CoinDesk, Reuters, Bloomberg, USA Today, Forbes, SeekingAlpha, Investopedia/AOL, France 24, Gulf News, US News & World Report, MSN, CSIS, DailyHive, InsideHalton, Kalshi, CME FedWatch, Pantheon Macroeconomics, Oxford Economics, Barclays, BMO Capital Markets, Federal Reserve FOMC Minutes, Bank of Japan, ECB
